citation_contexts: Given the availability of 1MHz sampling rate fault recorders [15] and the integrated optical sensors for voltage and current measurements [16], [17], the TW-based methods developed for transmission systems using wide area synchronized voltage measurements [18]...[truncated]

In recent years, lithium niobate (LN)-based electric ﬁeld sensors have attracted much attention because of their unique advantages such as small size, large operation bandwidth and high voltage handling capability [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11], [12].

The integrated LN electric ﬁeld sensor based on the chip-scale interferometer architecture is extensively studied since it enables abundant design freedom for different applications [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11], [12].
Hence, although the minimum measurable electric ﬁeld is as low as 20 mV/m in [10], the operation bandwidth is only 100 MHz.

Moreover, recent rapid technological advancements in high-frequency transient recorders and the integrated optical sensors facilitate the implementation these methods attractive and practical [8], [9].
The electro-chromatic effect has a response time of ~100 s, and it is suitable only for extremely low-frequency electric field measurement [12,13].
